name: Confluent Certified Developer for Apache Kafka

questions.q: A topic has 6 partitions. Which ordering guarantee does Kafka provide for its records?
options: Total order across the whole topic
Order is guaranteed only within each partition
Order is guaranteed per broker
No ordering guarantees at all
questions.explanation: Each partition is an ordered append-only log, but there is no coordination of order ACROSS partitions. Records that must be ordered relative to each other must share a partition — which is why they are given the same key.

questions.q: All events for a given customer must be processed in order. How is this achieved when producing?
options: Set acks=all
Use the customer ID as the message key so all of a customer's events hash to the same partition
Use a single-partition topic for all customers
Enable compression
questions.explanation: Keyed records are partitioned by murmur2(key) % numPartitions, so one customer's events always land on the same partition and stay ordered. A single partition would also work but destroys parallelism for all customers.

questions.q: A consumer group with 8 consumers reads a topic with 6 partitions. What happens?
options: Two consumers remain idle with no partitions assigned
All 8 consumers share partitions in round-robin per record
The group fails to start
Kafka automatically adds 2 partitions
questions.explanation: Within a group each partition goes to exactly one consumer, so at most 6 consumers can be active on 6 partitions — the extra 2 get nothing. Partition count is the group's maximum parallelism.

questions.q: A topic is configured with replication.factor=3 and min.insync.replicas=2, and producers use acks=all. What does this setup guarantee?
options: Writes succeed even if all three replicas are down
Every acknowledged write exists on at least 2 replicas, and the topic tolerates one broker failure without losing acknowledged data
Exactly-once delivery to consumers
Consumers read from follower replicas
questions.explanation: acks=all waits for all in-sync replicas, and min.insync.replicas=2 refuses writes if fewer than 2 are in sync. One broker can fail (ISR=2 still meets the minimum); acknowledged data always exists on ≥2 replicas. This is the canonical durability configuration.

questions.q: With min.insync.replicas=2 on an RF=3 topic, two brokers holding replicas of a partition go down. What do producers using acks=all experience?
options: Writes continue normally against the single remaining replica
Writes fail with NotEnoughReplicas errors until the ISR recovers
Data is silently redirected to another topic
Consumers also stop being able to read
questions.explanation: The ISR (1) is below min.insync.replicas (2), so acks=all produces are rejected — availability is sacrificed to protect durability. Reads of already-committed data can continue from the remaining leader.

questions.q: Which statement describes log compaction (cleanup.policy=compact)?
options: Records older than retention.ms are deleted regardless of key
Kafka eventually retains at least the most recent value for each key; a null-value record (tombstone) marks a key for deletion
The log is compressed with zstd
Only consumed records are removed
questions.explanation: Compaction is per-key retention of the latest value — turning a topic into a durable changelog (great for table-like state). Tombstones delete keys. Time/size deletion is the 'delete' policy; consumption never removes data.

questions.q: Two different applications, each with its own group.id, consume the same topic. How is data delivered?
options: The applications split the records between them
Each application (group) receives ALL records; within each group, partitions are divided among its consumers
Only the first group to subscribe receives data
Both groups must have identical consumer counts
questions.explanation: Consumer groups are independent — Kafka is pub-sub across groups and queue-like within a group. Each group maintains its own offsets in __consumer_offsets.

questions.q: Which combination of client settings secures a connection to a SASL/SCRAM-secured cluster over TLS?
options: security.protocol=PLAINTEXT with an ACL
security.protocol=SASL_SSL, sasl.mechanism=SCRAM-SHA-512, sasl.jaas.config with the credentials, plus a truststore for the broker certificates
compression.type=ssl
enable.idempotence=true
questions.explanation: SASL_SSL = SASL authentication over a TLS-encrypted channel; the mechanism and JAAS config carry the SCRAM credentials, and the truststore validates the brokers. ACLs then authorize the authenticated principal.

questions.q: A consumer application is denied with a TopicAuthorizationException although the topic exists. What is the most likely cause?
options: The topic has too many partitions
The client's principal lacks the required ACLs (e.g., Read on the topic and Read on the consumer group)
The consumer uses the wrong compression codec
auto.offset.reset is unset
questions.explanation: Authorization errors are ACL problems: a consuming principal needs Read on the topic AND Read on its group. Authentication succeeded (otherwise the error would be an authentication failure).

questions.q: What replaaced ZooKeeper's role in modern Kafka clusters (Kafka 4.x)?
options: An external etcd cluster
KRaft — an internal Raft-based controller quorum managing cluster metadata
A relational database
Schema Registry
questions.explanation: KRaft mode has Kafka manage its own metadata via a Raft consensus quorum of controllers — no external ZooKeeper. Client APIs are unaffected; it's a cluster architecture change.

questions.q: Partitions are added to an existing topic whose producers use keyed messages. What is the consequence?
options: Nothing — Kafka rebalances old data automatically
New records for a key may map to a DIFFERENT partition than that key's older records, breaking per-key ordering across the boundary
All old data is deleted
Producers must restart before any records flow
questions.explanation: Partitioning is hash(key) % numPartitions — changing numPartitions changes the mapping. Existing data stays where it was, so a key's history spans partitions and ordered consumption per key is broken across the change. Plan partition counts up front for keyed topics.

questions.q: What are Kafka message headers for?
options: Determining which partition a record goes to
Carrying metadata (correlation IDs, trace context, schema hints) alongside the key and value without affecting partitioning
Storing the record's offset
Compressing the payload
questions.explanation: Headers are key/value metadata attached to records — used for tracing, routing hints, and provenance. Partitioning uses the message key, not headers.

questions.q: What does enable.idempotence=true on a producer actually prevent?
options: Duplicates caused by the application calling send() twice
Duplicates caused by the producer's own retries after transient failures, while preserving per-partition ordering
Duplicates on the consumer side after rebalances
All duplicates end-to-end
questions.explanation: Idempotence gives each producer session sequence numbers so broker-side retry duplicates are discarded. It does NOT dedupe application-level re-sends nor consumer-side reprocessing — those need transactions or idempotent consumers.

questions.q: A consume-transform-produce application must achieve exactly-once semantics from input topic to output topic. What is required?
options: enable.idempotence=true alone
Kafka transactions: a transactional.id producer sending output AND committing consumed offsets in the same transaction, with downstream consumers using isolation.level=read_committed
acks=all with retries=0
Manual commitSync after each record
questions.explanation: EOS for read-process-write means the output records and the input offset commit are atomic — that's sendOffsetsToTransaction inside a transaction. read_committed consumers then ignore aborted attempts. Idempotence alone only covers producer retry duplicates.

questions.q: A consumer with enable.auto.commit=true crashes AFTER auto-commit ran but BEFORE it finished processing the last polled batch. What happens to those records?
options: They are redelivered after restart
They are lost to this group — the committed offset already moved past them
They are moved to a dead letter queue
The broker reprocesses them
questions.explanation: Auto-commit can commit offsets for records not yet processed, creating an at-most-once window. To get at-least-once, commit manually AFTER processing (commitSync/commitAsync).

questions.q: A consumer processing takes up to 10 minutes per batch, and the group keeps rebalancing with 'consumer left the group' messages. Which setting is being violated?
options: session.timeout.ms — heartbeats are missing
max.poll.interval.ms — too much time passes between poll() calls; raise it or reduce max.poll.records
auto.offset.reset
linger.ms
questions.explanation: Heartbeats run on a background thread, so session.timeout isn't the issue — the poll-interval timer is. If poll() isn't called within max.poll.interval.ms (default 5 min), the consumer is evicted. Raise the limit, shrink batches, or speed up processing.

questions.q: When does auto.offset.reset=earliest take effect?
options: On every consumer restart
Only when the group has no valid committed offset for a partition (new group, expired offsets, or out-of-range)
Whenever lag exceeds a threshold
Every time a rebalance happens
questions.explanation: auto.offset.reset is the FALLBACK position. With valid committed offsets, consumption always resumes from them — earliest/latest only applies when no usable offset exists.

questions.q: Which producer configuration change increases throughput at the cost of a little latency?
options: acks=0
Increase linger.ms (e.g., 5–100 ms) and batch.size so larger, better-compressed batches are sent
Decrease buffer.memory
max.in.flight=1
questions.explanation: linger.ms deliberately waits to fill batches; bigger batches amortize request overhead and compress better — the standard throughput knob. acks=0 trades DURABILITY (not just latency) and isn't a batching improvement.

questions.q: In Schema Registry, subject 'orders-value' uses BACKWARD compatibility. What does registering a new schema version require, and which side upgrades first?
options: New schema must be readable by the OLD schema; producers upgrade first
Consumers using the NEW schema must be able to read data written with the PREVIOUS schema (e.g., deleted fields / added optional fields); consumers upgrade first
Schemas can never change
Both sides must upgrade simultaneously
questions.explanation: BACKWARD = new reader, old data. Consumers deploy the new schema first and can still read what current producers write; then producers may upgrade. FORWARD is the reverse (producers first); FULL is both.

questions.q: How does a consumer know which Avro schema a record was written with, using the Confluent wire format?
options: The full schema is embedded in every message
Each message carries a schema ID; the deserializer fetches (and caches) that schema from Schema Registry
The topic name encodes the schema
Schemas are guessed from the bytes
questions.explanation: The wire format is magic byte + 4-byte schema ID + payload — compact messages, with the schema itself stored once in the registry and cached client-side after first fetch.

questions.q: A producer intermittently logs NotLeaderForPartitionException but messages eventually arrive. What is happening?
options: The topic is corrupt and must be recreated
A retriable error: partition leadership moved (broker restart/rebalance); the producer refreshes metadata and retries automatically
The producer's serializer is broken
The message exceeded max.request.size
questions.explanation: Leadership changes are normal transient cluster events — classic RETRIABLE errors the client handles with retries + metadata refresh. Serialization and record-too-large errors are non-retriable and would fail every attempt.

questions.q: A consumer hits a record that always throws SerializationException, blocking the partition. What is the standard remedy?
options: Restart the consumer until it works
Catch/handle the poison record: skip past its offset (or use an error-tolerant deserializer) and publish it to a dead-letter topic for analysis
Delete the topic
Reduce fetch.max.bytes
questions.explanation: Deserialization failures are non-retriable — the same bytes fail forever, so retrying blocks the partition. Skip-and-DLQ keeps the pipeline flowing while preserving the bad record for investigation.

questions.q: Which tool shows a consumer group's current offset, log-end offset, and lag per partition from the command line?
options: kafka-topics.sh --describe
kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group <name>
kafka-console-consumer.sh --from-beginning
kafka-configs.sh --describe
questions.explanation: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe lists each partition's CURRENT-OFFSET, LOG-END-OFFSET and LAG (plus member assignments) — also the tool for --reset-offsets. kafka-topics.sh describes topic structure, not group positions.

questions.q: Where is producer-side compression applied, and what maximizes its benefit?
options: Per record on the broker; benefit is fixed
Per batch on the producer (compression.type); larger batches (linger.ms/batch.size) compress better
Only consumers can compress
Compression requires Schema Registry
questions.explanation: The producer compresses whole batches before sending; brokers typically store them compressed and consumers decompress. Because compression works on batch payloads, better batching directly improves ratio and throughput.

questions.q: Which API creates topics, alters configurations, and lists consumer-group offsets programmatically?
options: Producer API
AdminClient (Admin API)
Streams API
Connect REST API
questions.explanation: AdminClient is the management API for cluster resources: topics, configs, ACLs, consumer groups and their offsets. The Connect REST API manages connectors only.

questions.q: A Kafka Streams application reads a topic with 8 partitions. The team deploys 10 instances (1 stream thread each) for throughput. What happens?
options: All 10 instances process data
Only 8 instances get tasks — parallelism is capped by input partitions; 2 instances stay idle
The app fails to start
Kafka adds partitions automatically
questions.explanation: Streams creates one task per input partition; tasks are the unit of parallelism distributed across instances/threads sharing the application.id. Beyond 8 active workers there is nothing to assign.

questions.q: Which abstraction models 'the current price of each product, updated over time'?
options: KStream — every record is an independent event
KTable — a changelog where each key holds its latest value
A windowed KStream
A repartition topic
questions.explanation: Latest-value-per-key semantics is exactly a KTable: new records for a key UPDATE the table. A KStream would treat every price change as a separate immutable event.

questions.q: A stream must be enriched with a small reference dataset (200 rows) on a key different from the stream's key, without repartitioning. Which construct fits?
options: KTable join (co-partitioned)
GlobalKTable join — fully replicated to each instance and joinable on any key via a key mapper
Windowed stream-stream join
Merge of two KStreams
questions.explanation: GlobalKTables are replicated in full to every instance, so joins don't require co-partitioning and can use an arbitrary join key. Perfect for small lookup data; regular KTable joins need same key + same partition count.

questions.q: What backs a Kafka Streams state store for fault tolerance?
options: A relational database configured by the user
A compacted changelog topic in Kafka; after failure, state is restored by replaying it (standby replicas shorten recovery)
A snapshot file uploaded to S3
Nothing — state is lost on failure
questions.explanation: Every state store writes its updates to a changelog topic. On task migration/failure the store is rebuilt from that topic; num.standby.replicas maintains warm copies to cut restore time.

questions.q: Count user clicks in fixed, non-overlapping 5-minute buckets. Which window type is this?
options: Hopping window (size 5 min, advance 1 min)
Tumbling window of 5 minutes
Session window with 5-minute gap
Sliding window
questions.explanation: Tumbling = fixed size, no overlap (each event in exactly one window). Hopping overlaps (size + smaller advance); session windows are activity-based with an inactivity gap; sliding relates records within a time difference.

questions.q: Group user activity into sessions that end after 30 minutes of inactivity. Which window applies?
options: Tumbling 30-minute window
Session windows with a 30-minute inactivity gap
Hopping window advancing 30 minutes
No windowing needed
questions.explanation: Session windows are dynamic: they extend while events keep arriving and close when the gap (30 min) elapses without activity — precisely the sessionization use case.

questions.q: What configuration turns on exactly-once processing in a Kafka Streams application?
options: enable.idempotence=true
processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2
isolation.level=read_committed
acks=all
questions.explanation: Streams wraps its consume-process-produce cycle (including changelog writes) in transactions when processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 is set — the one-line EOS switch. The other settings are pieces Streams manages internally.

questions.q: Before a stream-stream join, one stream is re-keyed with selectKey(). What does Streams do to make the join correct?
options: Fails at startup
Writes the re-keyed stream through an internal repartition topic so equal keys land in the same task
Broadcasts all records to every task
Ignores the new key
questions.explanation: After a key change, records with the same new key may sit in different partitions. Streams inserts a repartition topic to shuffle them so join/aggregation tasks see all records for a key. (Joins also require equal partition counts — co-partitioning.)

questions.q: By default, which notion of time drives Kafka Streams windowing?
options: Wall-clock time when the record is processed
Event time from the record's timestamp (customizable with a TimestampExtractor)
Broker disk-flush time
The consumer's poll time
questions.explanation: Streams windows on record timestamps (event time), so out-of-order and late records still fall into their correct windows (within the grace period). Processing time would make results depend on when the app happens to run.

questions.q: A windowed aggregation emits multiple intermediate updates per window, but the sink should receive only one final result per window. What is the idiomatic solution?
options: Poll less often
Use suppress() to hold updates until the window closes (grace period elapsed), emitting only the final value
Set linger.ms high on the producer
Use a GlobalKTable
questions.explanation: Streams refines window results continuously; suppress(untilWindowCloses) buffers them and forwards only the final result when the window plus grace period ends.

questions.q: How does a Kafka Streams application scale out at runtime?
options: It cannot scale without redeployment
Start additional instances with the SAME application.id — tasks and their state migrate automatically to the new members
Change group.id per instance
Increase replication factor
questions.explanation: application.id doubles as the consumer group id: new instances join the group, the tasks rebalance across members, and state stores are rebuilt/migrated (changelogs, standbys). Same mechanism in reverse handles instance loss.

questions.q: What is a Kafka Streams application, operationally?
options: A server process installed on each Kafka broker
A standard Java application using a client library — deployed like any app (VMs, Docker, Kubernetes), with no processing cluster to install
A plugin inside ZooKeeper
A managed Confluent Cloud-only service
questions.explanation: Streams is a library, not a cluster: the topology runs inside your own JVM processes, coordinated through Kafka itself. Deployment is ordinary application deployment — which is exactly why it fits Docker/Kubernetes pipelines.

questions.q: Data must be copied continuously from a PostgreSQL database into Kafka, and from Kafka into Elasticsearch, with minimal custom code. What is the recommended approach?
options: Write a custom producer and consumer application
Kafka Connect: a source connector for PostgreSQL and a sink connector for Elasticsearch, both configuration-driven
Kafka Streams with JDBC calls
A cron job with the console producer
questions.explanation: This is Kafka Connect's exact purpose: existing connectors handle offsets, retries, scaling, and schemas via configuration. Custom clients re-implement all of that; Streams is for processing, not system integration.

questions.q: Where does a DISTRIBUTED Kafka Connect cluster store connector configurations, source offsets, and status?
options: Local files on each worker
In internal Kafka topics (config, offset, and status topics) shared by the worker group
In ZooKeeper
In Schema Registry
questions.explanation: Distributed mode keeps its state in Kafka itself, which is what makes workers stateless and fault-tolerant — any worker can pick up work. Standalone mode is the one using local files.

questions.q: How is a connector created and managed in a distributed Connect cluster?
options: Editing server.properties and restarting brokers
Via the Connect REST API — POST the connector config; use it also for status, pause/resume, restart, delete
Only through ZooKeeper CLI
By placing a file on each worker
questions.explanation: Distributed Connect is managed at runtime over its REST interface (e.g., POST /connectors, GET /connectors/<name>/status). Standalone workers take properties files at startup instead.

questions.q: What is the relationship between a connector and its tasks?
options: They are the same thing
The connector is the logical job definition; it splits the work into tasks (bounded by tasks.max) that run distributed across workers — the actual unit of parallelism
Tasks manage workers
One task always equals one topic
questions.explanation: Connector = coordination + config; tasks = the parallel data-copying units. Effective parallelism is also limited by the source's divisible units (tables, files) or the topic's partitions for sinks.

questions.q: A JDBC source connector must write Avro records with schemas managed centrally. What configuration achieves this?
options: The JDBC connector always writes Avro
Set value.converter=io.confluent.connect.avro.AvroConverter with value.converter.schema.registry.url — converters are independent of the connector
Use StringConverter
Enable errors.tolerance=all
questions.explanation: Converters translate Connect's internal records to bytes and are configured separately from the connector logic — the same connector can emit Avro, Protobuf, JSON Schema, or plain JSON depending on converter settings.

questions.q: Field 'ssn' must be masked and records routed to a per-region topic as they flow through a connector — no external processing step. What mechanism does Connect provide?
options: A custom Kafka Streams job
Single Message Transforms (SMTs) chained in the connector config (e.g., MaskField + a routing transform)
Broker interceptors
Schema Registry compatibility rules
questions.explanation: SMTs apply lightweight per-record transformations inside the connector pipeline. For heavier logic (joins, aggregation) you'd use a stream processor between source and sink instead.

questions.q: A sink connector keeps dying on malformed records. It must skip them but keep them for later inspection. Which configuration applies?
options: errors.tolerance=none
errors.tolerance=all with errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name (and context headers enabled) — failed records go to a DLQ topic
tasks.max=1
key.converter=StringConverter
questions.explanation: Tolerance 'all' keeps the task alive past bad records, and the DLQ topic (a SINK-connector feature) captures each failure — with reasons in headers when enabled — instead of silently dropping it.

questions.q: Compared with a polling JDBC source, what advantage does log-based CDC (e.g., Debezium) provide?
options: It requires no connector at all
It reads the database's transaction log: captures every change including DELETEs, with low latency and minimal load on tables
It works without the database running
It converts data to Avro automatically
questions.explanation: Polling misses deletes, adds query load, and is bounded by the poll interval. Reading the transaction log streams every committed row change (with before/after images), which is why CDC is preferred for database replication into Kafka.

questions.q: One worker in a 3-worker distributed Connect cluster crashes. What happens to the tasks it was running?
options: They are lost until the worker returns
The remaining workers rebalance and take over the failed worker's connectors/tasks, resuming from offsets stored in Kafka
All connectors restart from the beginning of history
The cluster stops accepting REST calls
questions.explanation: Because config/offsets/status live in Kafka topics, workers are interchangeable: the group rebalances the failed worker's tasks and they resume from their last committed source offsets.

questions.q: Which statement about standalone Connect workers is TRUE?
options: They are recommended for production for simplicity
They run in a single process with offsets in a local file — no fault tolerance or horizontal scaling; fine for development and testing
They store offsets in Kafka automatically
They cannot run source connectors
questions.explanation: Standalone = one process, local offset storage, no failover — a dev/test convenience. Production runs distributed mode for fault tolerance, scaling, and REST management.

questions.q: Producer callback logic (success metrics, error handling) must be unit-tested without a broker. Which tool fits?
options: Testcontainers Kafka
MockProducer — records sent messages for assertions and can simulate send failures (errorNext)
kafka-console-producer
TopologyTestDriver
questions.explanation: MockProducer is the in-memory test double from kafka-clients: assert on history(), drive callbacks with simulated success/failure — no broker, no network, deterministic.

questions.q: Which combination tests a Kafka Streams topology synchronously without any running Kafka cluster?
options: MockConsumer + MockProducer
TopologyTestDriver with TestInputTopic and TestOutputTopic
kafka-consumer-groups.sh
A three-broker Testcontainers cluster
questions.explanation: TopologyTestDriver executes the topology in-process: pipe records via TestInputTopic, assert results from TestOutputTopic, and query state stores directly — fast, deterministic unit tests for Streams logic.

questions.q: A tumbling-window aggregation with a grace period must be tested deterministically, including late-arrival behavior. How?
options: Thread.sleep() in the test until the window closes
Use TopologyTestDriver's explicit time control — pipe records with chosen timestamps and advance wall-clock time to trigger window closing
Run it in production and watch
Windows cannot be tested
questions.explanation: The test driver lets you SET record timestamps and advance time programmatically, so window boundaries, grace periods, and suppress() emissions are exercised deterministically — no sleeps, no flakiness.

questions.q: Which risks are NOT covered by mocks and the TopologyTestDriver, and therefore need an integration test with a real broker (e.g., Testcontainers)?
options: Branching logic in a topology
Security handshakes, real Schema Registry (de)serialization, transactions, and rebalance behavior
A map() transformation
Callback code on send
questions.explanation: Interaction-level concerns — auth/TLS, registry round-trips, transactional semantics, group coordination — only exist against real infrastructure. Pure logic belongs in fast unit tests; keep both layers.

questions.q: How should schema changes be prevented from breaking production consumers?
options: Deploy and monitor for errors
Check candidate schemas against the registry's compatibility mode in CI (compatibility REST endpoint or schema-registry:test-compatibility Maven goal) so incompatible changes fail the build
Disable compatibility checking
Use StringSerializer everywhere
questions.explanation: Schema Registry can validate a proposed schema against the subject's BACKWARD/FORWARD/FULL rules before anything is deployed — wiring that check into CI catches breaking changes at build time.

questions.q: What design choice makes producer/consumer application code easy to unit test?
options: Instantiating KafkaProducer inside every method
Injecting the Producer/Consumer interface into the logic class, so tests pass MockProducer/MockConsumer and production passes real clients
Making all methods static
Testing only via the CLI
questions.explanation: Dependency injection against the client INTERFACES is what lets the same logic run with mocks in tests. Hard-coded client construction forces integration tests for everything.

questions.q: What exactly is consumer lag for a partition?
options: The time since the consumer last polled
Log end offset minus the consumer's current/committed offset — how many records behind the consumer is
The number of rebalances per hour
Bytes in the fetch buffer
questions.explanation: Lag counts unconsumed records between the partition's newest offset and the consumer's position. Persistent lag growth is THE signal that consumption can't keep up with production.

questions.q: How do Kafka clients expose their metrics for collection?
options: They write CSV files
Via JMX MBeans (per client/topic/node), typically scraped by agents like the Prometheus JMX exporter
Only through Confluent Control Center
Metrics must be coded manually
questions.explanation: Producer, consumer, Streams, and Connect all register JMX metrics out of the box; monitoring stacks scrape them. Streams adds thread/task/store metrics with configurable recording levels.

questions.q: Which producer metric indicates messages that ultimately FAILED delivery after exhausting retries, and what should its value be?
options: record-retry-rate; high values are normal
record-error-rate; it should be ~0 and alerted on otherwise
batch-size-avg; should be 0
compression-rate-avg; should be 1
questions.explanation: record-error-rate counts definitive send failures — data loss from the app's perspective, so alert on any sustained non-zero value. record-retry-rate is the early-warning cousin: transient trouble being retried.

questions.q: Lag grows steadily on exactly ONE of a topic's 12 partitions. What is the most likely cause to investigate first?
options: The whole cluster is undersized
A hot key skewing traffic to that partition (or one stuck/slow consumer instance) — check key distribution and that assignee's health
Schema Registry is down
linger.ms is too high
questions.explanation: Uniform problems raise lag everywhere; single-partition lag points to skew (one key getting disproportionate traffic) or the specific consumer assigned to it. Key cardinality/hashing is the classic culprit.

questions.q: Duplicates appear downstream after consumer crashes, even though processing is correct. The metrics show frequent rebalances. What is the explanation?
options: Kafka randomly duplicates data
At-least-once consumption: records processed but not yet committed before the crash/rebalance are redelivered — make handling idempotent or adopt transactions/EOS
The producer's compression corrupts batches
Lag is negative
questions.explanation: Between processing and offset commit there is always a redelivery window under at-least-once. Frequent rebalances widen it. Fixes: idempotent writes keyed on record identity, or exactly-once processing where applicable.

questions.q: How can telemetry (counting, tracing) be added to every produced and consumed record WITHOUT modifying business code?
options: Fork the Kafka client library
Configure ProducerInterceptor / ConsumerInterceptor classes via interceptor.classes — hooks that observe records on send and on consume
Add a broker plugin
Enable DEBUG logging
questions.explanation: Interceptors are the client-side extension point for cross-cutting observability (Confluent's monitoring interceptors use it). They see records in the send/consume path with zero business-code changes.

questions.q: A trace ID must follow one business transaction from the HTTP request, through the producer, into a consumer three services away. Where does the trace context travel in Kafka?
options: In the message key (replacing the business key)
In message HEADERS (e.g., W3C/OpenTelemetry trace context), propagated producer → consumer
In the topic name
It cannot cross Kafka
questions.explanation: Headers are the metadata channel: tracing libraries inject context on produce and extract it on consume, keeping the trace intact — without abusing the key (which would change partitioning!).

questions.q: A producer starts throwing TimeoutException and bufferpool-wait-time climbs. What is happening?
options: Consumers are lagging
The producer generates data faster than the cluster accepts it (or brokers are slow/unreachable): batches back up in buffer.memory until send() blocks and times out
Log compaction deleted the topic
The consumer group rebalanced
questions.explanation: buffer.memory fills when the I/O thread can't drain batches fast enough — from broker slowness, network issues, or plain overproduction. Check request-latency and cluster health; consumer lag is unrelated to producer buffering.

questions.q: Which end-to-end health check catches pipeline delay that per-component metrics can miss?
options: Broker CPU usage
End-to-end latency: compare the record's event timestamp with the time it is finally processed by the last consumer
Topic partition count
Number of connectors deployed
questions.explanation: Each hop can look healthy while queueing between hops accumulates delay. Measuring event-time-to-final-processing spans the entire pipeline — the metric that reflects what the business actually experiences.

questions.q: Which statements about Kafka partitions are TRUE? (Select all that apply)
options: Partition count can be increased after topic creation
Partition count can be decreased after topic creation
Increasing partitions changes which partition new keyed records hash to
A partition's records are totally ordered by offset
questions.explanation: Partitions can be added (never removed), each partition is an ordered log, and because partitioning is hash(key) % numPartitions, adding partitions remaps keys — breaking per-key ordering across the change.

questions.q: A topic has replication.factor=3. Where do producers and consumers connect for a given partition?
options: Any replica, chosen randomly
The partition LEADER handles all produces and (by default) consumes; followers only replicate
Producers use the leader, consumers use followers
The controller broker handles all traffic
questions.explanation: All client traffic for a partition goes through its leader; followers fetch to stay in sync and exist for fault tolerance. (Follower fetching exists as an opt-in rack-aware feature, but the default and exam answer is leader.)

questions.q: What happens when a consumer group has 3 consumers subscribed to two topics with 4 partitions each (8 total)?
options: Only 3 partitions are consumed
The 8 partitions are distributed across the 3 consumers (e.g., 3/3/2); all partitions are consumed
Each consumer gets all 8 partitions
The group fails — consumer count must divide partition count
questions.explanation: The assignor spreads all subscribed partitions over available members; uneven division is fine. Every partition has exactly one owner within the group.

questions.q: A compacted topic receives: (k1,v1), (k2,v2), (k1,v3), (k2,null). After full compaction, what remains?
options: All four records
(k1,v3) only — k2's tombstone eventually removes k2 entirely
(k1,v1) and (k2,v2)
(k1,v3) and (k2,null) forever
questions.explanation: Compaction keeps the latest value per key: k1→v3. The null value for k2 is a tombstone marking deletion; after the tombstone retention period it disappears too, leaving only k1's latest value.

questions.q: Which ACLs does a consumer application using group 'billing-app' need to read topic 'payments'? (Select all that apply)
options: Read on topic 'payments'
Read on group 'billing-app'
Write on topic 'payments'
Alter on the cluster
questions.explanation: Consuming needs READ on the topic AND READ on the consumer group (offset commits are group operations). Write/Alter are producer/admin permissions.

questions.q: Given acks=1, what data-loss window exists?
options: None — acks=1 is fully durable
If the leader acknowledges then fails BEFORE followers replicate the record, the write is lost on failover
Every produce is lost
Only consumers can lose data
questions.explanation: acks=1 waits only for the leader's write. A leader crash before follower replication elects a follower without the record — the acknowledged write vanishes. acks=all + min.insync.replicas closes this window.

questions.q: Which producer settings primarily trade LATENCY for THROUGHPUT? (Select all that apply)
options: linger.ms increased from 0 to 50
batch.size increased
compression.type=zstd
acks changed from all to 0
questions.explanation: Waiting to fill bigger batches (linger.ms, batch.size) and batch compression all add a little latency to gain a lot of throughput. acks=0 trades DURABILITY, not just latency — a different axis.

questions.q: A consumer must never lose a record but can tolerate occasional duplicates. Which commit strategy is correct?
options: enable.auto.commit=true with a short interval
Process the records FIRST, then commit offsets manually (commitSync/commitAsync) — at-least-once
Commit before processing — at-most-once
Never commit offsets
questions.explanation: Commit-after-processing means a crash re-delivers the uncommitted batch (duplicates possible, no loss) — at-least-once. Auto-commit can commit mid-batch before processing completes, opening a loss window.

questions.q: What is required for a producer to participate in transactions? (Select all that apply)
options: A transactional.id set in the producer config
initTransactions() called before the first transaction
isolation.level=read_committed on the producer
Calls wrapped in beginTransaction()/commitTransaction()
questions.explanation: Transactional producers need a stable transactional.id, an initTransactions() handshake, and explicit begin/commit (or abort). isolation.level is a CONSUMER setting — it controls whether readers see uncommitted records.

questions.q: A producer sends a 5 MB message and gets RecordTooLargeException. Which options genuinely address this? (Select all that apply)
options: Raise max.request.size (producer) and message.max.bytes (broker/topic) if justified
Use the claim-check pattern: store the blob externally, send a reference
Enable compression and hope it fits
Set retries=10
questions.explanation: Size limits exist at producer and broker level — raising both works but has operational costs; claim check is the architectural fix; compression MAY bring the payload under the limit (it's applied before the size check on batches). Retrying a non-retriable error changes nothing.

questions.q: In the Confluent Schema Registry, subject 'orders-value' is set to FORWARD compatibility. What does a valid new schema version guarantee, and who upgrades first?
options: New consumers read old data; consumers first
Data written with the NEW schema is readable by consumers still on the OLD schema; producers upgrade first
Nothing about ordering of upgrades
Both directions forever
questions.explanation: FORWARD = old readers handle new data (e.g., new fields are added that old readers ignore). Producers can therefore deploy the new schema first. BACKWARD is the mirror image (consumers first).

questions.q: Why does a consumer use the schema ID embedded in each message rather than the 'latest' schema from the registry?
options: The latest schema is always wrong
Records must be decoded with the WRITER'S schema (the exact version used to serialize); topics legitimately contain records written with different versions
Schema IDs are faster to type
The registry has no 'latest' concept
questions.explanation: Avro decoding requires the writer's schema; the per-message ID identifies exactly which version wrote that record. Compatibility rules then let the reader map it to its own (reader) schema.

questions.q: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe shows one partition with CURRENT-OFFSET 1000, LOG-END-OFFSET 5200. What does this mean?
options: The consumer is 1000 records behind
The consumer has a lag of 4200 records on that partition
The topic has 1000 records
The consumer is ahead of the log
questions.explanation: Lag = LOG-END-OFFSET − CURRENT-OFFSET = 5200 − 1000 = 4200 unconsumed records. Watching whether this number grows or shrinks over time is the core consumption health check.

questions.q: A Streams app computes 'orders per customer per 1-hour tumbling window'. Which internal Kafka topics does it create? (Select all that apply)
options: A changelog topic backing the windowed aggregation state store
A repartition topic if the stream is re-keyed to customer ID before aggregating
A DLQ topic automatically
Nothing — Streams keeps state only in memory
questions.explanation: Stateful operations are backed by compacted changelog topics (fault tolerance), and re-keying before an aggregation inserts a repartition topic so each task sees all records for its keys. DLQs are not automatic in Streams.

questions.q: What is the difference between a KStream-KTable join and a KStream-KStream join?
options: No difference
Stream-table joins enrich each event with the table's CURRENT value (no window); stream-stream joins pair events occurring within a time WINDOW
Stream-table joins require a window
Stream-stream joins never need co-partitioning
questions.explanation: A table lookup is point-in-time — no window needed. Two event streams have no 'current value', so their join must bound how far apart in time matching events may occur (windowed). Both require co-partitioning.

questions.q: A Streams instance crashes. Its tasks had 50 GB of RocksDB state. What minimizes downtime for those tasks on other instances?
options: Nothing can help; state is lost
num.standby.replicas ≥ 1 — warm copies of the state on other instances allow near-instant task migration instead of replaying 50 GB of changelog
Bigger batch.size
exactly_once_v2
questions.explanation: State is recoverable from changelogs, but replaying 50 GB takes time. Standby replicas continuously mirror state on other instances so failover just promotes the standby.

questions.q: In Kafka Streams, what happens when a record arrives 5 minutes late (event time) for a 1-hour window with a 10-minute grace period?
options: It is dropped
It updates its correct (already-formed) window because it is within the grace period
It creates a new window
Streams crashes
questions.explanation: Event-time processing places records into the window their timestamp belongs to; within the grace period, late records still update that window's result. Beyond grace, they are dropped.

questions.q: Which Connect worker settings/behaviors are DISTRIBUTED-mode only? (Select all that apply)
options: REST API management of connectors
Config/offset/status stored in internal Kafka topics
Automatic task rebalancing when a worker dies
Running a connector at all
questions.explanation: Distributed mode adds REST management, Kafka-backed shared state, and rebalancing/fault tolerance. Standalone can still RUN connectors — just without any of those properties.

questions.q: A JDBC sink connector reads Avro records (Schema Registry) and writes to Postgres. Which config is essential?
options: value.converter=StringConverter
value.converter=io.confluent.connect.avro.AvroConverter with value.converter.schema.registry.url pointing at the registry
errors.tolerance=none
tasks.max=1 always
questions.explanation: The converter must match how the data was written: AvroConverter + registry URL turns the wire format (magic byte + schema ID) back into structured records with schemas — which the JDBC sink needs to build SQL.

questions.q: Kafka Connect source offsets (e.g., 'which rows have been read') are stored where in distributed mode?
options: In __consumer_offsets like consumer groups
In the Connect cluster's offset storage TOPIC (connect-offsets)
In a local file on each worker
In Schema Registry
questions.explanation: Source progress is Connect-specific state kept in the connect-offsets internal topic (distributed mode). __consumer_offsets stores consumer-group offsets — sink connectors use that mechanism for their consumer side.

questions.q: During peak load, a sink connector falls behind. tasks.max=1 and the topic has 8 partitions. What is the FIRST scaling step?
options: Add more partitions
Raise tasks.max (up to 8, the partition count) so multiple tasks consume in parallel across workers
Add brokers
Reduce the topic's replication factor
questions.explanation: One task = one consumer doing all the work. Raising tasks.max lets Connect run parallel tasks (bounded by partitions for sinks). Partitions/brokers/RF are cluster-side changes that don't fix a self-imposed parallelism cap.

questions.q: Which assertions can a TopologyTestDriver-based test make? (Select all that apply)
options: Values read from a TestOutputTopic match expectations
Contents of a state store at a given point
Window results after advancing the driver's wall-clock time
Actual network latency to the broker
questions.explanation: The driver runs the topology in-process: outputs, state stores, and time-driven behavior (windows, punctuators) are all synchronously inspectable. There IS no broker or network — that's the point.

questions.q: A test must verify the application's error handling when a produce fails with a broker exception. Which approach is deterministic?
options: Run against production and wait for failures
MockProducer with errorNext(new TimeoutException(...)) to force the send's callback/future to fail
Disconnect the network cable
Set acks=0
questions.explanation: MockProducer lets the test inject an exact failure into the next send, driving the error path deterministically — no broker, no flakiness, repeatable in CI.

questions.q: Where should schema-compatibility verification run to prevent breaking consumers?
options: Only manually before major releases
In CI on every change to a schema — validating the candidate against the subject's compatibility mode via the registry's check endpoint or the schema-registry Maven plugin
In the consumer at runtime
Nowhere; the registry blocks bad schemas at produce time anyway
questions.explanation: The registry does reject incompatible registrations at runtime — but discovering that during deployment is late. CI checks catch it at build time. Runtime consumer failures are exactly what this prevents.

questions.q: Which metrics indicate the PRODUCER is struggling, before data loss occurs? (Select all that apply)
options: Rising record-retry-rate
Rising request-latency-avg
bufferpool-wait-time climbing (buffer.memory pressure)
records-lag-max
questions.explanation: Retries, broker latency, and buffer pressure are the producer's early-warning chain (ending in TimeoutExceptions). records-lag-max is a CONSUMER metric — the other side of the pipeline.

questions.q: Consumer lag on all partitions grows during business hours and shrinks overnight. Processing each record takes 80 ms, and the topic gets 100 records/second across 4 partitions with 4 consumers. What is the bottleneck?
options: The brokers
Per-record processing time: 4 consumers × (1000/80) ≈ 50 records/s capacity vs 100/s incoming — processing must get faster or more parallel (more partitions + consumers, or batch/async the work)
The network
Schema Registry
questions.explanation: Capacity math: each consumer handles ~12.5 records/s at 80 ms/record; four handle ~50/s against 100/s inflow — lag grows exactly when traffic exceeds capacity. Fix processing speed or raise parallelism (needs more partitions).

questions.q: A team wants per-record processing counts and trace propagation WITHOUT modifying application business code. Which Kafka client feature does this?
options: Broker-side plugins
Producer/Consumer interceptors registered via interceptor.classes
A custom partitioner
Higher fetch.max.bytes
questions.explanation: Interceptors hook the send/consume path for cross-cutting concerns (metrics, tracing headers). Partitioners route records; fetch sizes are throughput tuning; brokers can't see application semantics.

questions.q: End-to-end latency (event created → final consumer processed) is 45 s although every service's own metrics look healthy. Where does the time usually hide, and how is it found?
options: In DNS resolution
As QUEUEING between hops (consumer lag at intermediate topics) — found by comparing record event timestamps with processing time at each stage, or watching lag per intermediate consumer group
In TLS handshakes
In log rotation
questions.explanation: Each hop can be fast while records WAIT in topics between hops. Event-timestamp-to-processing-time deltas per stage (or per-group lag) expose which queue is absorbing the 45 seconds.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. The cluster has 3 brokers. Broker 2 crashes. Shortly afterwards broker 3 also crashes. What happens to producers writing to this topic with the configuration shown?
questions.exhibit:
Topic: payments
  partitions: 3
  replication.factor: 3
  min.insync.replicas: 2

Producer:
  acks=all
options: Writes succeeded with one broker down, but with two brokers down they fail with NotEnoughReplicasException
Writes continue normally with any single broker alive because the leader can always acknowledge alone
All writes stopped as soon as the first broker crashed
The topic is deleted once fewer brokers than the replication factor remain
optionNotes: Correct. With acks=all, the leader acknowledges only when all in-sync replicas have the record, and min.insync.replicas=2 requires at least 2 ISR members. One broker down leaves 2 in-sync replicas — fine. Two down leaves only the leader (1 < 2), so produce requests fail with NotEnoughReplicas(AfterAppend)Exception.
min.insync.replicas=2 explicitly forbids acknowledging with a lone leader when acks=all.
Losing one of three replicas still satisfies min.insync.replicas=2, so writes kept working.
Topics are never deleted due to broker failures; partitions just become under-replicated or unavailable.
questions.explanation: acks=all + min.insync.replicas=2 + RF=3 is the standard durability recipe: it tolerates one broker failure while guaranteeing every acknowledged record exists on at least two brokers. The trade-off is availability: a second failure makes the partition reject writes rather than risk data loss.

questions.q: A topic has 3 partitions. A consumer group subscribed to it starts a 4th consumer instance. What is the resulting assignment?
options: One consumer remains idle: partitions cannot be shared, so at most 3 consumers in the group receive an assignment
Each of the 4 consumers gets 75% of one partition
The 4th consumer triggers automatic creation of a 4th partition
All 4 consumers receive all records (broadcast)
optionNotes: Correct. Within a group, a partition is consumed by exactly one member. Three partitions can occupy at most three consumers; the fourth stays idle as a hot standby that takes over on a rebalance.
Partitions are the atomic unit of assignment — they are never split among group members.
Consumers never change topic partition counts; that is an administrative operation.
Broadcast happens across different groups, not within one group.
questions.explanation: Partition count caps a consumer group's parallelism. Extra members beyond the partition count sit idle (useful only as standby), which is why partition count is sized with target consumer parallelism in mind.

questions.q: An application produces click events for user sessions to a 6-partition topic and requires that all events of one session are consumed in the order they were produced. How is this achieved?
options: Use the session ID as the record key so all of a session's events land in the same partition
Set acks=all on the producer
Use a single consumer with a large fetch size
Enable log compaction on the topic
optionNotes: Correct. Kafka guarantees order only within a partition. Keying by session ID makes the default partitioner hash every event of that session to the same partition, preserving per-session order while keeping 6-way parallelism across sessions.
acks controls durability of acknowledgement, not routing or ordering.
One consumer still reads 6 partitions in interleaved order; cross-partition order remains undefined.
Compaction controls retention of latest values per key; it does not create ordering guarantees.
questions.explanation: Ordering in Kafka is a per-partition property. The idiom for entity-level ordering is: key by the entity ID, let the hash partitioner give each entity a stable partition, and order within the entity follows automatically.

questions.q: Where does a Kafka consumer group's committed offset information live by default?
options: In the internal __consumer_offsets topic on the Kafka cluster
In a local file on each consumer host
In ZooKeeper znodes, in all modern Kafka versions
In the first record of each partition
optionNotes: Correct. Committed offsets are written to the compacted internal topic __consumer_offsets, keyed by (group, topic, partition) — replicated and durable like any other topic data.
Local files would be lost with the host and could not survive consumer replacement.
ZooKeeper-based offsets are legacy (pre-0.9); modern clients commit to the internal topic, and new clusters run without ZooKeeper entirely (KRaft).
Offsets are consumer-side bookkeeping, not part of partition data.
questions.explanation: __consumer_offsets is a compacted internal topic holding the latest committed offset per group-topic-partition. Compaction keeps exactly the newest commit per key, which is all a group needs to resume.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. After compaction has run on this topic, which records can a new consumer reading from the beginning still receive?
options: userA=Faro and userC=Braga; the older userA record is removed and the tombstone eventually deletes userB entirely
All five records, because compaction never deletes anything
Only the tombstone, because compaction keeps the newest record overall
userA=Lisbon and userB=Porto, because compaction keeps the oldest record per key
optionNotes: Correct. Compaction retains at least the latest value per key: userA's Lisbon record is superseded by Faro; userC keeps Braga. A null value is a tombstone — it marks userB deleted and, after delete.retention.ms, the tombstone itself is purged, removing userB from the log.
Compaction exists precisely to delete superseded values per key.
Compaction is per key, not global; each key's newest value survives.
It keeps the newest per key, not the oldest.
questions.explanation: Log compaction turns a topic into a durable latest-value-per-key changelog: superseded values are removed, and null-value tombstones delete keys (remaining visible for delete.retention.ms so consumers can observe the deletion). Ideal for state snapshots like the tables backing Kafka Streams.

questions.q: The leader replica of a partition fails. unclean.leader.election.enable is false (the default). Which replica may become the new leader, and what does this protect against?
options: Only a replica from the in-sync replica set; this prevents losing records that were acknowledged by the failed leader
Any replica, including lagging ones; this maximizes availability
No replica — the partition stays offline until the original leader returns
The replica on the broker with the lowest ID, regardless of sync state
optionNotes: Correct. With unclean election disabled, leadership can pass only to an ISR member — a replica guaranteed to have every committed record. This preserves acknowledged data at the cost of availability if the whole ISR is gone.
Electing an out-of-sync replica is exactly the 'unclean' election that the false setting forbids, because it silently discards committed records.
If other ISR members exist, one takes over immediately; the partition goes offline only when no ISR replica survives.
Broker ID plays no role in eligibility; sync state does.
questions.explanation: The ISR is the set of replicas caught up with the leader. Restricting elections to it (unclean.leader.election.enable=false) is the consistency-over-availability stance: committed records survive leader failure, but losing the entire ISR renders the partition unavailable rather than lossy.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What behaviour does this producer configuration provide?
options: Retries can no longer introduce duplicates or reordering within a partition: each record is written exactly once to the log
Exactly-once delivery to consumers, end to end, with no further configuration
The producer stops batching records and sends one at a time
Duplicates are still written on retry, but consumers deduplicate them automatically
optionNotes: Correct. Idempotence gives each producer a PID and per-partition sequence numbers; brokers discard duplicates from retries and, with in-flight ≤ 5, preserve ordering. Acknowledged records land exactly once in the partition log.
Idempotence covers the produce path only. End-to-end exactly-once additionally needs transactions on the producer and read_committed consumers (or Streams EOS).
Batching (batch.size/linger.ms) is unaffected by idempotence.
The whole point is that brokers reject the duplicates; consumers never see them.
questions.explanation: The idempotent producer eliminates the classic retry hazards — duplicates and reordering — using producer IDs and sequence numbers checked by the broker. It is produce-side exactly-once; cross-application exactly-once requires the transactional API on top.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. Which delivery semantics does this consumer implement?
options: At-least-once: a crash between process() and commitSync() causes the batch to be reprocessed after restart
At-most-once: records can be lost but never reprocessed
Exactly-once, because commitSync is atomic with processing
No delivery guarantee, because auto-commit is disabled
optionNotes: Correct. Offsets are committed only after processing succeeds. If the consumer dies mid-batch, the last commit still points before the batch, so those records are delivered again — duplicates are possible, loss is not. process() should therefore be idempotent.
At-most-once is the opposite pattern: commit before processing, risking loss instead of duplication.
commitSync commits offsets; it cannot atomically include an external database write. Exactly-once needs transactions or idempotent sinks.
Disabling auto-commit and committing manually after processing is precisely how a guarantee (at-least-once) is established.
questions.explanation: Commit-after-process = at-least-once; commit-before-process = at-most-once. The gap between an external side effect and the offset commit can never be closed by the consumer API alone — that is what idempotent processing or the transactional/EOS machinery addresses.

questions.q: A consumer processes records in a loop that sometimes takes 8 minutes per batch. With max.poll.interval.ms at its default of 300000 (5 minutes), what happens, and what are the two standard remedies?
options: The group coordinator evicts the consumer and rebalances its partitions; fix by raising max.poll.interval.ms or reducing max.poll.records so each poll's work fits the interval
The broker throttles the consumer's fetches until it speeds up; fix by raising fetch.max.bytes
Nothing — max.poll.interval.ms only applies to consumers using auto-commit
The consumer crashes with an OutOfMemoryError; fix by increasing heap
optionNotes: Correct. Failing to call poll() within max.poll.interval.ms tells the coordinator the consumer is stuck: it is removed from the group and its partitions are reassigned (often causing duplicate processing). Remedies: allow a longer interval, or shrink the per-poll workload (max.poll.records) — or hand off heavy work to another thread while the poll loop keeps polling.
There is no such throttling mechanism tied to poll cadence.
The liveness check applies regardless of the commit mode.
Slow processing does not by itself exhaust memory.
questions.explanation: max.poll.interval.ms is the processing-liveness contract: poll at least this often or be presumed dead. Long-running batches must either fit within it (fewer records per poll) or the interval must be raised — otherwise rebalance storms and duplicate work follow.

questions.q: A producer writes records with StringSerializer for both key and value. A consumer reads the topic configured with IntegerDeserializer for the value. What happens at the consumer?
options: Deserialization fails (SerializationException) because the bytes are not a 4-byte integer encoding
Kafka converts the string to an integer automatically on the broker
The consumer receives the string's hash code as an integer
The record is silently skipped by the client library
optionNotes: Correct. Brokers store and return opaque bytes; interpretation happens entirely in the client. UTF-8 string bytes are not a valid fixed-width integer encoding, so the deserializer throws, typically breaking the poll loop unless handled (or unless an error-tolerant wrapper is used).
Brokers never inspect or convert payload bytes — serialization is a client-side contract.
Deserializers parse bytes; they do not fall back to hashing.
Plain consumers do not skip undeserializable records by default; the exception surfaces to the application.
questions.explanation: Serialization is an agreement between producers and consumers that Kafka itself never enforces — brokers ship bytes. Mismatched serializers therefore fail at deserialization time, which is exactly the failure mode schema registries and contracts exist to prevent.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What is the difference between the two produce calls?
options: A is asynchronous (send returns a Future and batching proceeds in the background); B blocks the calling thread until the broker acknowledges or fails, at a heavy throughput cost
A discards delivery errors, while B is the only way to detect them
A sends without a key; B includes the key
They are identical because get() is a no-op on completed futures
optionNotes: Correct. send() always hands the record to the background sender and returns a Future immediately; calling get() waits synchronously for the RecordMetadata (or exception). Blocking per record forfeits batching and pipelining — throughput drops dramatically.
A does not discard errors — attach a callback (send(record, callback)) to observe results without blocking.
Keys come from the ProducerRecord, not from how the future is consumed.
get() materially changes behaviour: it blocks until the send completes.
questions.explanation: The producer is asynchronous by design; per-record .get() turns it into a slow synchronous client. The idiomatic way to handle outcomes at full speed is the completion callback, reserving blocking waits for cases that truly need per-record confirmation.

questions.q: Records are produced to a 4-partition topic with a null key. How does the default partitioner distribute them (Kafka 2.4+)?
options: Sticky batching: records fill a batch to one random partition, then switch to another, evening out over time
All null-key records go to partition 0
The broker rejects records without a key
Strictly one record per partition in round-robin order
optionNotes: Correct. The sticky partitioner sends consecutive null-key records to one partition until its batch is full or sent, then picks another — better batching (fewer, fuller requests) than per-record round-robin, with even long-run distribution.
Null-key traffic is spread across partitions, not pinned to one.
Keys are optional; null keys simply forgo per-key placement.
Per-record round-robin was the old behaviour; it fragmented batches and was replaced.
questions.explanation: With no key there is no placement contract, so the partitioner optimizes for throughput: the sticky strategy batches consecutive records to the same partition and rotates between batches, balancing load without sacrificing batch efficiency.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What guarantee does this combination provide that the idempotent producer alone does not?
options: Atomic consume-transform-produce: the output records and the input offset commit succeed or abort together, and read_committed consumers never see aborted data
Faster producing, because transactions skip broker acknowledgements
Encryption of the records inside the transaction
Ordering across different partitions of the output topic
optionNotes: Correct. The transactional API binds the produced records and the consumed offsets into one atomic unit: on failure both are aborted, so reprocessing produces no observable duplicates. read_committed consumers only ever see committed transactional data — this is Kafka's exactly-once processing pattern.
Transactions add coordinator round-trips; they cost a little latency rather than saving it.
Transactions are about atomicity, not confidentiality — encryption is TLS/at-rest configuration.
Kafka never orders across partitions; transactions do not change that.
questions.explanation: Idempotence deduplicates a single producer's retries; transactions extend that to atomic multi-partition writes tied to the consumed offsets. Together with read_committed readers this closes the consume-transform-produce loop into exactly-once semantics — the machinery behind Streams EOS.

questions.q: A producer sends many small records, and profiling shows one network request per record. Which two settings are tuned together to improve throughput via batching, and what is the trade-off?
options: batch.size (bytes per batch) and linger.ms (how long to wait for a batch to fill); the trade-off is up to linger.ms of added latency per record
fetch.min.bytes and session.timeout.ms; the trade-off is slower group rebalances
acks and retries; the trade-off is durability
max.poll.records and heartbeat.interval.ms; the trade-off is consumer lag
optionNotes: Correct. A non-zero linger.ms makes the producer wait briefly so batches (bounded by batch.size) fill before sending — fewer, larger requests, better compression, higher throughput, at the cost of that small wait. linger=0 sends as fast as possible with tiny batches.
Those are consumer/group settings, unrelated to producer batching.
acks/retries govern durability semantics, not batch formation.
Also consumer-side settings; the question is about the producer's request pattern.
questions.explanation: Producer batching is the linger.ms/batch.size dial: trade microseconds-to-milliseconds of latency for dramatically fewer requests and better compression. It is the first tuning knob for small-record, high-rate producers.

questions.q: In Kafka Streams, what is the semantic difference between reading a topic as a KStream versus as a KTable?
options: A KStream treats every record as an independent event; a KTable treats records as upserts, keeping only the latest value per key
A KStream is bounded; a KTable is infinite
KTables cannot be joined, only aggregated
A KStream stores data on disk while a KTable is memory-only
optionNotes: Correct. KStream = record stream: two records with the same key are two facts (e.g. two purchases). KTable = changelog view: a later record with the same key replaces the earlier one (e.g. current address), and null values delete the key.
Both abstractions are unbounded; the difference is interpretation, not size.
KTables join in several ways (table-table, stream-table enrichment).
Both are backed by topics; tables additionally materialize into (disk-backed RocksDB) state stores.
questions.explanation: The stream/table duality: a stream is a sequence of immutable events, a table is the evolving latest-state per key that such a sequence implies. Choosing the right reading of a topic (events vs upserts) drives which joins and aggregations make sense.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. Why does this topology create an internal repartition topic, and what would avoid it?
options: groupBy changes the grouping key, so records must be redistributed by customerId before counting; grouping by the existing key (groupByKey) — or keying the topic by customerId upstream — avoids the repartition
count() always requires a repartition topic regardless of keys
The topology is invalid; groupBy cannot be followed by count()
Repartition topics appear only when exactly-once is enabled
optionNotes: Correct. Aggregations are computed per key within partitions. Since the new key (customerId) does not match the current partitioning (by orderId), Streams inserts a repartition topic to co-locate each customer's records on one task. If the data were already keyed by customerId, groupByKey() would aggregate with no shuffle.
count() after groupByKey() on an unchanged key needs no repartitioning.
groupBy(...).count() is a perfectly standard aggregation.
EOS changes transactional behaviour, not shuffle topology.
questions.explanation: Any key-changing operation (groupBy, selectKey, map that alters the key) followed by a stateful operation forces a shuffle through an internal repartition topic, doubling the write path. Designing topic keys to match downstream aggregation keys is the main lever for avoiding it.

questions.q: A Kafka Streams application must guarantee that its stateful processing produces no duplicated results in its output topics, even across failures and restarts. Which single configuration enables this?
options: processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2
enable.auto.commit=false
cleanup.policy=compact on the output topics
num.stream.threads=1
optionNotes: Correct. exactly_once_v2 makes Streams wrap consuming, state-store updates (changelogs), and producing in Kafka transactions, so each input record's effects are committed atomically exactly once — the supported EOS switch for modern brokers.
Streams manages its own commits; this consumer flag is not how Streams EOS is configured.
Compaction shapes retention of the output; it neither prevents nor removes duplicated aggregate results.
Single-threading reduces parallelism but failures and restarts can still replay uncommitted work.
questions.explanation: Streams exposes exactly-once as one setting: processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2. Under the hood it is the transactional producer + offsets-in-transaction + read_committed pattern, applied automatically to the whole topology including state store changelogs.

questions.q: A Kafka Streams application consumes one input topic with 8 partitions. The team deploys 12 identical instances of the application. How many instances actively process data?
options: 8 — parallelism is bounded by the number of input partitions (tasks); the other 4 instances stay idle as standbys
12 — Streams splits partitions among all instances
1 — only the first instance receives tasks
12 — but each processes every record (broadcast)
optionNotes: Correct. Streams creates one task per input partition (8 tasks here) and distributes tasks across instances/threads. With more instances than tasks, the surplus get nothing to do — useful only as warm spares.
There are only 8 units of work to hand out; 12 instances cannot all be busy.
Tasks are balanced across available instances, not concentrated on one.
Within the application (one consumer group), records are partitioned, never broadcast.
questions.explanation: Task count = input partition count is the scaling law of Kafka Streams. Capacity planning starts with partitions: to use 12 instances productively, the input topic needs at least 12 partitions.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What does this Kafka Connect configuration do?
options: Polls the customers table and publishes rows with an id greater than the last one seen to the topic crm-customers — new rows are captured, but updates to existing rows are missed
Streams every change (inserts, updates, deletes) from the table using the database's transaction log
Copies records from the topic crm-customers into the customers table
Takes a one-time snapshot of the table and then stops
optionNotes: Correct. A JDBC source in incrementing mode tracks the highest id it has published and selects only rows above it on each poll. That captures inserts only; updates don't change id and are invisible (timestamp or timestamp+incrementing mode addresses that; true CDC needs a log-based connector such as Debezium).
Log-based change data capture is a different connector family; the JDBC source only polls with queries.
Topic-to-table is a sink connector; this is a source.
The connector keeps polling on its interval, continuously picking up new rows.
questions.explanation: JDBC source modes matter: incrementing = inserts only (monotonic column), timestamp = rows whose timestamp advanced, timestamp+incrementing = both. Query-based capture always trails and misses deletes — the standard reason to step up to log-based CDC.

questions.q: How does Kafka Connect in distributed mode store its configuration, progress, and status — and how is a connector's throughput scaled?
options: In internal Kafka topics (config, offsets, status); throughput scales by raising tasks.max so the connector splits work across more tasks on the worker cluster
In a shared filesystem visible to all workers; throughput scales by adding CPU to one worker
In ZooKeeper; throughput scales by adding partitions to the source database
In each worker's local RocksDB; throughput cannot be scaled without downtime
optionNotes: Correct. Distributed Connect keeps connector configs, source offsets, and statuses in three internal topics, so any worker can take over any task. Parallelism comes from tasks: tasks.max lets the connector partition its work (tables, topic partitions) into up to that many tasks balanced across workers.
No shared filesystem is involved; state lives in Kafka itself.
Connect does not use ZooKeeper for its state.
Task counts can be changed by reconfiguring the connector at runtime via the REST API.
questions.explanation: Distributed Connect is a stateless worker cluster whose shared memory is Kafka: config/offset/status topics make workers interchangeable and failover automatic. tasks.max is the parallelism dial, subject to how far the specific connector can actually split its workload.

questions.q: Each record flowing through a sink connector needs a field removed and the event routed to a topic-per-region before delivery. The team debates implementing this inside Connect. What is the appropriate guidance?
options: Light per-record modifications like these are what Single Message Transforms (SMTs) are for; anything heavier — joins, aggregations, external lookups — belongs in a stream processor before Connect
Connect cannot modify records at all; a Streams app must rewrite the data first
Fork the connector's source code and add the logic to its put() method
SMTs support aggregations and joins, so all processing can move into the connector
optionNotes: Correct. SMTs are stateless, per-record transformations chained in connector config (e.g. ReplaceField to drop a field, RegexRouter/TimestampRouter to redirect topics) — a perfect fit here. They deliberately cannot hold state, so multi-record logic belongs in Kafka Streams/ksqlDB upstream.
SMTs exist precisely to modify records in the Connect pipeline.
Forking a connector for config-expressible logic creates a maintenance burden SMTs avoid.
SMTs see one record at a time; stateful operations are out of their scope by design.
questions.explanation: The dividing line: SMT for stateless single-record tweaks declared in configuration; stream processing for anything stateful or multi-record. Keeping that boundary keeps connectors reusable and pipelines debuggable.

questions.q: A team wants fast unit tests for a Kafka Streams topology — verifying that given input records, the expected output records and state are produced — without starting any broker. Which tool is designed for this?
options: TopologyTestDriver with TestInputTopic and TestOutputTopic
A production-like three-broker cluster in a staging environment
MockProducer from kafka-clients
kafka-console-consumer run in a shell script
optionNotes: Correct. TopologyTestDriver executes the topology synchronously in-process: pipe records in via TestInputTopic, read results from TestOutputTopic, inspect state stores, and even advance the mocked wall clock for windowing tests — milliseconds per test, no cluster.
Real clusters are for integration tests; they are far too slow and flaky to be the unit-test loop.
MockProducer stubs the plain producer client; it knows nothing about Streams topologies.
Console tools are for manual inspection, not automated assertions.
questions.explanation: TopologyTestDriver is the Streams unit-testing harness: deterministic, in-JVM, broker-free, with control over event time and access to state stores. Save real brokers (Testcontainers/embedded) for integration coverage.

questions.q: A service class wraps a KafkaProducer and adds business logic before sending. How can its behaviour — including how it handles send failures — be unit-tested without a broker?
options: Inject a MockProducer, which records sent records for assertions and can be told to complete sends successfully or with an exception
Point the real producer at localhost and assert nothing is thrown
Use reflection to verify the producer's private buffer
Skip unit tests; only integration tests can cover producer code
optionNotes: Correct. MockProducer implements the Producer interface in memory: tests assert on history() to check what was sent (topic, key, value) and use errorNext(exception) to simulate broker failures, exercising the error-handling paths deterministically.
A real client with no broker just fails on metadata — it proves nothing about the logic and makes tests environment-dependent.
Reflection against internals is brittle and tests the client library, not your code.
The clients ship mock implementations precisely so this code can be unit-tested.
questions.explanation: Program against the Producer/Consumer interfaces and inject MockProducer/MockConsumer in tests: sends are captured for assertion and failures are scriptable — fast, deterministic coverage of both happy and error paths.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What does this output indicate, and which metric quantifies it?
questions.exhibit:
$ kafka-consumer-groups --bootstrap-server broker:9092 \
    --describe --group invoice-processor

TOPIC     PARTITION  CURRENT-OFFSET  LOG-END-OFFSET  LAG
invoices  0          182300          195450          13150
invoices  1          182410          195600          13190
invoices  2          182150          195510          13360
options: The group is falling behind: consumers have processed ~13k fewer records than produced per partition; consumer lag (e.g. records-lag-max) is the metric to monitor and alert on
