The brokers are dropping records; the metric is under-replicated-partitions
The producers are throttled; the metric is produce-throttle-time
The topic is over-partitioned; the metric is partition-count
optionNotes: Correct. LAG = log-end-offset − committed offset: the number of records waiting per partition. Growing lag means consumption is slower than production — the primary consumer health signal, exposed as records-lag/records-lag-max on the consumer's fetch metrics (and by external lag monitors).
Nothing here concerns replication; records are safely stored and simply not yet consumed.
Producer throttling is unrelated to a consumer group's committed offsets.
Even lag across three partitions says nothing about having too many partitions.
questions.explanation: Consumer lag is the queue depth of Kafka consumption. Watching its trend (per partition, via kafka-consumer-groups, JMX records-lag-max, or dedicated exporters) distinguishes a healthy steady state from a group that will never catch up and needs scaling or faster processing.

questions.q: After a deployment, invoices intermittently fail to appear downstream, yet application logs show no errors: the producer code ignores send outcomes. Which producer-side JMX metrics would have revealed the problem, and what code change makes failures visible?
options: record-error-rate (and record-retry-rate) rising above zero; add a send callback (or check the Future) so failed sends are logged and handled
consumer records-lag-max; switch the consumer to read_committed
under-min-isr-partition-count on the broker; raise replication factor
request-latency-avg; enable compression to lower it
optionNotes: Correct. The producer exposes record-error-rate (records that ultimately failed) and record-retry-rate (transient failures being retried). Fire-and-forget send() without a callback silently drops those errors on the floor — the callback (metadata, exception) -> {...} is where failures must be logged, alerted, or routed for retry.
Consumer lag cannot detect records that were never successfully produced.
Broker ISR health is one possible cause, but the observability gap described is on the producer, and the records may fail for many other reasons.
Latency metrics do not surface silent record loss.
questions.explanation: A producer that ignores send results is blind to its own failures. record-error-rate is the canary in monitoring, and the completion callback is the code-level fix — every production producer should have both.

sections.title: Apache Kafka Fundamentals
objectives: Explain Kafka's data model: topics, partitions, offsets, and ordering
Describe brokers, replication, leaders/followers, and the ISR
Explain message keys, key hashing, and partition assignment
Describe consumer groups and how partitions are distributed
Explain data retention and log compaction
Compare delivery semantics: at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once
Describe Kafka security fundamentals (encryption, authentication, authorization)
sections.notes:
Topics, partitions, and offsets

  • A topic is a named stream of records, split into partitions. Each partition is an ordered, immutable, append-only log; each record in it gets a sequential offset.
  • Ordering is guaranteed only WITHIN a partition — never across partitions of a topic. If a use case needs total order for a set of records (e.g. all events of one customer), those records must share a partition (⇒ same key).
  • Consumers track their position as the next offset to read per partition; reading does NOT remove data (Kafka is a log, not a queue) — many independent consumer groups read the same data.
  • Partition count = the topic's maximum parallelism for a consumer group. Partitions can be added later (breaks key→partition mapping for existing keys!) but never removed.
  • Records are key/value pairs plus a timestamp and optional headers (metadata for tracing, routing, schema hints — not used for partitioning).

Brokers, replication, and the ISR

  • A cluster is a set of brokers; each partition lives on brokers as one leader replica plus replication.factor − 1 follower replicas. All produce/consume traffic goes through the leader (followers replicate by fetching).
  • The ISR (in-sync replicas) is the set of replicas fully caught up with the leader. On leader failure, a new leader is elected from the ISR — no data loss if the ISR had the data.
  • min.insync.replicas (broker/topic config): the minimum ISR size for writes with acks=all to succeed. The classic durable setup: RF=3, min.insync.replicas=2, acks=all — tolerates one broker down while never acknowledging a write held by fewer than 2 replicas; producers get NotEnoughReplicasException if the ISR shrinks below 2.
  • Unclean leader election (electing a non-ISR replica) trades data loss for availability; it is disabled by default.

Message keys and partition assignment

  • Producer partitioning: key present → partition = murmur2(key) % numPartitions — the same key ALWAYS lands on the same partition (ordering per key). Key = null → sticky partitioning: the producer fills a batch to one random partition, then switches (efficient round-robin-ish distribution).
  • A producer can also set the partition explicitly or plug a custom Partitioner.
  • Design consequence: choose keys with enough cardinality to spread load; a low-cardinality key (e.g. country) creates hot partitions. Changing partition count remaps keys — plan partitions up front for keyed topics.

Consumer groups

  • Consumers with the same group.id form a consumer group: each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. More consumers than partitions ⇒ the extras sit idle.
  • Different groups are independent — each receives every record (pub-sub across groups, queue-like within a group).
  • Group membership is coordinated by a broker acting as group coordinator; consumers heartbeat, and joins/leaves/failures trigger a rebalance (partition reassignment). Committed offsets are stored in the internal __consumer_offsets topic.

Retention and log compaction

Cleanup policyBehaviorUse case
delete (default)Segments older than retention.ms (default 7 days) or beyond retention.bytes are deleted — regardless of consumptionEvent streams, telemetry
compactKeeps at least the latest record per key; older values for the same key are eventually removed; a null value ("tombstone") deletes the keyChangelogs, table-like state (latest value per entity)
compact,deleteBoth: compaction plus time/size-based expiryBounded changelogs

Retention is per topic; consumers must keep up or re-read from what still exists (auto.offset.reset decides where a group starts with no valid offset: earliest or latest).

Delivery semantics

SemanticsHow it happensCost
At-most-onceCommit/ack BEFORE processing (or producer with acks=0) — failures lose data, never duplicateData loss possible
At-least-onceProcess THEN commit; producer retries — failures re-deliverDuplicates possible ⇒ consumers should be idempotent
Exactly-onceIdempotent producer + transactions (Kafka-to-Kafka), consumer isolation.level=read_committedSome latency/throughput overhead; EOS applies to Kafka↔Kafka pipelines (external sinks still need idempotency)

Kafka security basics

  • Encryption in transit: TLS between clients and brokers (and inter-broker); listeners defined per protocol (PLAINTEXT, SSL, SASL_SSL…).
  • Authentication: SASL mechanisms — PLAIN (username/password, over TLS!), SCRAM-SHA-256/512, GSSAPI (Kerberos), OAUTHBEARER — or mutual TLS (client certificates).
  • Authorization: ACLs per principal on resources (topic, group, cluster) and operations (Read, Write, Create…). A typical client needs Read on the topic + Read on its consumer group, or Write on the topic to produce.
  • Clients configure this with security.protocol, sasl.mechanism, sasl.jaas.config, plus truststore/keystore settings.

KRaft and the control plane

Kafka's metadata (topics, partitions, leaders, configs) was historically coordinated by ZooKeeper; modern Kafka uses KRaft — brokers/controllers run the Raft consensus protocol internally, removing ZooKeeper entirely (default since Kafka 4.0). For a developer the client APIs are unchanged; know that cluster metadata and controller election are internal (a controller quorum) and that administration happens via the Admin API/CLI tools.

Exam tip: the most-tested fundamentals: ordering only within a partition; same key → same partition; consumers > partitions ⇒ idle consumers; RF=3 + min.insync.replicas=2 + acks=all tolerates exactly one broker loss; compaction keeps the latest value per key (tombstones delete). Memorize these cold.

sections.title: Apache Kafka Application Development
objectives: Configure and tune producers (acks, batching, retries, idempotence)
Implement exactly-once semantics with idempotent producers and transactions
Configure and tune consumers (poll loop, offset commits, rebalancing)
Use serializers/deserializers and Schema Registry with Avro/JSON/Protobuf
Handle retriable and non-retriable errors in clients
Use the Admin API and command-line tools
Apply compression and batching for throughput
sections.notes:
The producer: send path and key configs

send() is asynchronous: records go through the (key/value) serializer → partitioner → an in-memory batch per partition (buffer.memory), and a background I/O thread ships batches. A callback (or the returned Future) reports success (RecordMetadata) or failure.

ConfigMeaning
acks0 = fire-and-forget; 1 = leader has written it; all = all in-sync replicas have it (durable; pair with min.insync.replicas)
retries / delivery.timeout.msAutomatic retry of transient failures; delivery.timeout.ms bounds the whole attempt
enable.idempotenceBroker de-duplicates producer retries (sequence numbers) — no duplicates from retries, per-partition ordering preserved; default true in modern clients
max.in.flight.requests.per.connectionConcurrent unacked requests; with idempotence ≤5 ordering is still guaranteed
linger.ms / batch.sizeWait up to linger.ms to fill batches up to batch.size — the main throughput/latency trade-off knobs
compression.typenone / gzip / snappy / lz4 / zstd — compresses whole batches

Idempotence and transactions (EOS)

  • Idempotent producer = no duplicates from retries on one producer session. It does NOT dedupe application-level re-sends.
  • Transactions: set a stable transactional.id, then initTransactions() → beginTransaction() → send(...) + sendOffsetsToTransaction(...) → commitTransaction(). Enables atomic consume-transform-produce: output records and consumed-offset commits succeed or abort together.
  • Downstream consumers must set isolation.level=read_committed to skip aborted records (default is read_uncommitted).
  • Exactly-once applies Kafka-to-Kafka; writing to an external DB still needs idempotent writes or its own transaction coordination.

The consumer: poll loop, commits, rebalancing

while (true) {
  ConsumerRecords<K,V> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(250));
  for (ConsumerRecord<K,V> rec : records) process(rec);
  consumer.commitSync();          // manual at-least-once
}
  • Offset commits: enable.auto.commit=true commits every auto.commit.interval.ms during poll (risk: commits records not yet processed → possible loss on crash). Manual commitSync() (blocking, retried) or commitAsync() (fast, no retry) AFTER processing gives at-least-once.
  • Liveness: heartbeats (background thread) within session.timeout.ms keep membership; max.poll.interval.ms caps time between polls — exceed it (slow processing) and the group evicts the consumer, causing a rebalance. Tune it, or lower max.poll.records.
  • Rebalancing: assignment strategies include range, round-robin, sticky, and cooperative-sticky (incremental — only moved partitions stop, no global stop-the-world). A ConsumerRebalanceListener lets you commit/cleanup on revocation.
  • auto.offset.reset (earliest/latest/none) applies only when the group has NO valid committed offset.
  • Consumers are not thread-safe — one consumer per thread; scale by adding consumers (up to partition count).

Serialization and Schema Registry

  • Producers serialize keys/values to bytes; consumers must deserialize with a compatible scheme. Common formats: Avro, JSON Schema, Protobuf (all supported by Confluent SerDes) — plus primitives (String, Long…).
  • Schema Registry stores versioned schemas per subject (default: <topic>-key / <topic>-value); the wire format is a magic byte + schema ID + payload — consumers fetch schemas by ID and cache them.
  • Compatibility modes govern schema evolution:
ModeNew schema can be used to read…Safe changesUpgrade order
BACKWARD (default)data written with the previous schemadelete fields, add optional fields (with defaults)Consumers first
FORWARDold schema reads data written with the new oneadd fields, delete optional fieldsProducers first
FULLboth directionsonly add/remove optional fields with defaultsAny order

*_TRANSITIVE variants check against ALL previous versions, not just the latest.

Error handling: retriable vs non-retriable

Retriable (client retries automatically)Non-retriable (fix required)
LeaderNotAvailable / NotLeaderForPartition, NetworkException, NotEnoughReplicas, timeouts — transient cluster eventsSerializationException, RecordTooLargeException (> max.request.size / message.max.bytes), AuthorizationException, InvalidTopicException

Producer: rely on retries + idempotence for the left column; catch and handle (log/DLQ/alert) the right. Consumer: a poison record that fails deserialization will fail on EVERY poll — catch it, skip past it (seek beyond, or use an error-handling deserializer) and route it to a dead-letter topic.

Admin API and CLI tools

  • AdminClient: programmatic create/describe/delete topics, alter configs, list consumer groups and their offsets/lag, manage ACLs.
  • CLI equivalents: kafka-topics.sh (--create --partitions --replication-factor / --describe), kafka-console-producer.sh / kafka-console-consumer.sh (--from-beginning, --group), kafka-consumer-groups.sh (--describe shows per-partition LAG; --reset-offsets), kafka-configs.sh.

Compression and batching

  • Compression is set on the producer (whole-batch compression); brokers usually store batches as-is; consumers decompress. Bigger batches (higher linger.ms/batch.size) compress better — compounding throughput gains at a small latency cost.
  • zstd/lz4 are the modern favorites (good ratio/CPU balance); gzip best ratio, most CPU.

Exam tip: favorite questions — what acks=all requires (paired min.insync.replicas); idempotence stopping retry duplicates but transactions being needed for consume-transform-produce EOS; auto-commit's data-loss window vs manual commit-after-processing; max.poll.interval.ms evictions on slow processing; BACKWARD compatibility = consumers upgrade first; and the retriable/non-retriable split.

sections.title: Apache Kafka Streams
objectives: Describe the Streams processing topology, tasks, and threading model
Choose between KStream, KTable, and GlobalKTable
Use stateful operations: aggregations, state stores, and internal topics
Apply windowing (tumbling, hopping, sliding, session) and time semantics
Join streams and tables correctly
Configure processing guarantees (at-least-once, exactly-once)
sections.notes:
Topology, tasks, and threads

  • Kafka Streams is a client library (plain Java app — no cluster to install; deploy like any app, e.g. in Docker/Kubernetes). A topology is the graph of sources → processors → sinks built with the DSL (StreamsBuilder) or Processor API.
  • The topology is split into tasks — one per input partition — the unit of parallelism. Tasks are distributed over num.stream.threads and over all app instances sharing the same application.id (which is also the consumer group id). Max parallelism = input partition count; extra instances/threads idle.
  • Scaling out = start another instance with the same application.id: tasks (and their state) migrate automatically.

KStream, KTable, GlobalKTable

KStreamKTableGlobalKTable
SemanticsEvent stream — every record is an independent fact ("orders placed")Changelog — latest value per key ("current price per product"); updates overwriteLike KTable but FULLY replicated to every instance
Reads topicAll partitions split across tasksAll partitions split across tasksEvery instance reads ALL partitions
Join keyingStream-stream and stream-table joins require co-partitioning (same key, same partition count)Join on any key (via mapper) — no co-partitioning needed
Typical useTransformations, enrichment eventsAggregation results, reference data that updatesSmall, broadly needed lookup data

Stateful processing: state stores and internal topics

  • Stateful operations (count, aggregate, reduce, joins, windowing) keep local state stores (RocksDB on disk by default, in-memory optional).
  • Every state store is backed by a compacted changelog topic in Kafka — the source of truth. On instance failure/migration, state is restored by replaying the changelog; num.standby.replicas keeps warm copies to shorten failover.
  • Re-keying operations (groupBy, selectKey before an aggregation/join) create internal repartition topics so records with the same new key meet in the same task.
  • State is queryable in-process (interactive queries) for serving lookups without an external DB.

Windowing and time

WindowShapeExample
TumblingFixed size, non-overlappingCount per 5 minutes
HoppingFixed size, overlapping (size + advance)5-min window advancing every 1 min
SlidingBound to record pairs within a time differenceJoins/aggregations over "within 30 s of each other"
SessionActivity-based, closed by an inactivity gapUser sessions with 10-min idle gap
  • Streams operates on event time by default (record timestamps; custom TimestampExtractor possible), not wall-clock arrival time.
  • Late/out-of-order records within the window's grace period still update it; results are continuously refined (use suppress() to emit only the final result when a window closes).

Joins

  • Stream–stream joins are always windowed (records must occur within the window) and support inner/left/outer.
  • Stream–table joins enrich events with the table's current value for the key (inner/left).
  • Table–table joins produce an updating joined table.
  • Except with GlobalKTables, join inputs must be co-partitioned: same key space and the SAME number of partitions — otherwise Streams throws or requires an explicit repartition.

Processing guarantees

  • processing.guarantee=at_least_once (default) — duplicates possible after failures.
  • processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 — wraps the consume-process-produce cycle (including state-store changelogs) in Kafka transactions; requires brokers ≥ 2.5, adds modest overhead. This is the one-line EOS switch for Streams apps.

Exam tip: classic questions — tasks = input partitions (adding instances beyond that does nothing); KTable = latest value per key (vs KStream = every event); GlobalKTable = fully replicated, no co-partitioning; state restored from changelog topics; exactly_once_v2 as the EOS setting; tumbling vs hopping vs session recognition from a scenario.

sections.title: Kafka Connect
objectives: Explain Kafka Connect's purpose: source and sink integration without custom code
Compare standalone and distributed worker deployments
Configure connectors and scale them with tasks
Choose converters and integrate Schema Registry
Apply Single Message Transforms
Configure error tolerance and dead letter queues
Describe CDC (change data capture) with source connectors
sections.notes:
What Connect is and when to use it

  • Kafka Connect is the framework for configuration-driven integration between Kafka and external systems: source connectors pull data INTO Kafka; sink connectors push data OUT of Kafka. No custom producer/consumer code — you deploy a connector plugin and give it a JSON/properties config.
  • Connect handles the hard parts for you: offset tracking (where the source left off / what the sink has written), scaling, fault tolerance, retries, schemas, and transformation hooks. Prefer it over hand-written glue code whenever a connector exists.

Workers: standalone vs distributed

StandaloneDistributed
ProcessesOne worker processCluster of workers sharing a group.id
Config/offsets storedLocal filesInternal Kafka topics (config, offsets, status)
Fault tolerance / scalingNone — the process IS the systemWork is rebalanced across workers; add workers to scale
ManagementProperties file at startupREST API (create/pause/resume/delete connectors, view status)
UseDev, testing, one-off local jobsAnything production

Connectors, tasks, and scaling

  • A connector instance is the logical job (e.g. "this database → these topics"); it divides work into tasks — the actual units that copy data, spread across workers.
  • tasks.max caps parallelism; the effective task count also depends on the source's dividable units (e.g. number of tables, or a JDBC source may cap at one task) and, for sinks, the topic's partitions.
  • Failed tasks can be restarted via the REST API (/connectors/<name>/status, /restart).

Converters and Schema Registry

  • Converters translate between Connect's internal data representation and the bytes in Kafka: key.converter / value.converter set per worker or per connector. Common: AvroConverter, ProtobufConverter, JsonSchemaConverter (all with ...converter.schema.registry.url), plain JsonConverter (optionally with embedded schemas), StringConverter.
  • Converters are chosen INDEPENDENTLY of the connector — the same JDBC source can write Avro or JSON depending on converter config. Mismatched converters between producer side and consumer side are the classic "deserialization garbage" root cause.

Single Message Transforms (SMTs)

  • Lightweight, per-record transformations applied in the connector pipeline (source: before writing to Kafka; sink: before writing to the target): rename/mask/insert fields, route records to different topics, cast types, extract keys, filter records.
  • Configured as a chain: transforms=mask,route with per-transform classes/settings. For anything heavier (joins, aggregations, lookups) use a stream processor (Kafka Streams) between source and sink instead of stretching SMTs.

Error handling and dead letter queues

  • errors.tolerance=none (default — task fails on a bad record) vs all (skip and continue).
  • Sink connectors can route failed records to a dead letter queue topic: errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name (+ context.headers.enable to attach failure reasons as headers). DLQs apply to sinks, not sources.
  • errors.retry.timeout enables retries of transient failures before tolerance/DLQ handling kicks in.

Change Data Capture (CDC)

  • CDC source connectors (e.g. Debezium) read a database's transaction log (not query polling) and publish every row-level INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE as an event — low latency, no load on tables, captures deletes.
  • Contrast with JDBC source polling (periodic SELECTs with an incrementing/timestamp column): simpler, but misses deletes, adds DB load, and has poll-interval latency.
  • CDC events conventionally carry before/after images; deletes are followed by tombstones for compacted topics — pairing naturally with KTable-style consumption.

Exam tip: distributed workers store config/offsets/status in internal Kafka topics and are managed over REST — standalone uses local files; that contrast is heavily tested. So are: tasks as the unit of parallelism (tasks.max), converters being independent of connectors, DLQs being a SINK feature, and log-based CDC vs polling trade-offs.

sections.title: Application Testing
objectives: Unit test producer and consumer logic with mocks
Test Kafka Streams topologies with TopologyTestDriver
Design integration tests against real (containerized/embedded) brokers
Test schema compatibility before deployment
sections.notes:
Unit testing producers and consumers

  • The Kafka clients library ships test doubles: MockProducer and MockConsumer — no broker needed.
  • MockProducer records everything your code sends (history()) and can simulate success or failure of sends (errorNext(exception)) — assert what was produced, to which topic/partition, with which key, and that callbacks/error paths behave.
  • MockConsumer lets tests seed assigned partitions, records, and end offsets, then verifies the poll-loop logic: processing, commit behavior, rebalance listener handling.
  • Structure application code so the Kafka client is injected — logic classes take a Producer<K,V>/Consumer<K,V> interface, tests pass the mock, production passes the real client.

Testing Kafka Streams topologies

TopologyTestDriver driver = new TopologyTestDriver(builder.build(), props);
TestInputTopic<String, Order> in =
    driver.createInputTopic("orders", stringSerde.serializer(), orderSerde.serializer());
TestOutputTopic<String, Long> out =
    driver.createOutputTopic("order-counts", stringSerde.deserializer(), longSerde.deserializer());

in.pipeInput("cust-1", order1);
in.pipeInput("cust-1", order2);
assertEquals(2L, out.readKeyValue().value);   // synchronous, deterministic
  • TopologyTestDriver executes the topology synchronously without any broker: pipe records into TestInputTopics, read results from TestOutputTopics, and query state stores directly (driver.getKeyValueStore(...)).
  • It controls time explicitly — advance wall-clock/event time to test windowing, grace periods, and suppression deterministically (no sleeps, no flakiness).

Integration testing with real brokers

  • Unit tests can't cover: real serialization against a Schema Registry, security handshakes, rebalancing behavior, transactions, connector configs. For those, run a REAL Kafka in the test: Testcontainers (Kafka container per test class — today's standard), embedded Kafka utilities, or a shared test cluster.
  • Keep them separate from unit tests (slower, resource-heavy); make topics unique per run; assert with consumers polling until a timeout rather than fixed sleeps.
  • Test failure modes deliberately: kill the container to observe retry/idempotence behavior; produce poison records to verify DLQ paths.

Testing schema evolution

  • Before deploying a producer with a changed schema, verify compatibility against the registry: the Schema Registry compatibility check REST endpoint (or Maven plugin schema-registry:test-compatibility) validates a candidate schema against the subject's configured mode (BACKWARD/FORWARD/FULL) — wire this into CI so incompatible changes fail the build, not production consumers.

Exam tip: map tool to layer — MockProducer/MockConsumer for client logic, TopologyTestDriver (with TestInputTopic/TestOutputTopic) for Streams topologies without a broker, Testcontainers/embedded Kafka for integration concerns, and registry compatibility checks in CI for schema changes. TopologyTestDriver's deterministic time control for window testing is a favorite question.

sections.title: Application Observability
objectives: Expose and collect client metrics (JMX) for producers, consumers, and Streams
Monitor consumer lag and alert on it
Track producer health: errors, retries, latency, throughput
Use logging, tracing, and interceptors for end-to-end visibility
Troubleshoot common client-side problems from metrics
sections.notes:
Client metrics via JMX

  • All Kafka clients (producer, consumer, Streams, Connect) expose metrics through JMX MBeans; scrape them with your monitoring stack (Prometheus JMX exporter, etc.). Metrics exist per client, per topic, and per node.
  • Kafka Streams adds thread/task/processor/state-store metrics (metrics.recording.level controls granularity); Connect exposes connector/task status and throughput metrics.
  • Watch BOTH sides: client metrics tell you how YOUR app behaves; broker-side metrics (under-replicated partitions, request handler idle %) tell you about the cluster — a developer is expected to read the client side fluently.

Consumer lag — the number-one signal

  • Lag = log end offset − committed/current offset, per partition: how far behind the consumer is. Growing lag means the app can't keep up (or stopped).
  • Sources: consumer metric records-lag-max, kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group app (shows CURRENT-OFFSET, LOG-END-OFFSET, LAG per partition), and external lag exporters (e.g. Burrow) for fleet-wide monitoring.
  • Alert on sustained growth, not momentary spikes. Remedies: faster processing (batch/parallelize the work), more consumers (up to partition count), more partitions (planned!), or tuning max.poll.records/processing pipeline.
  • Also watch rebalance metrics (rebalance rate, time-between-poll) — frequent rebalances often masquerade as lag problems.

Producer health metrics

MetricWhat it tells you
record-error-rateSends that ultimately FAILED (post-retries) — should be ~0; alert otherwise
record-retry-rateTransient trouble being retried — early warning of cluster/network issues
request-latency-avg/maxBroker round-trip time — rising latency precedes buffer exhaustion
batch-size-avg, records-per-request-avg, compression-rate-avgBatching efficiency — small batches = tune linger.ms/batch.size
buffer-available-bytes / bufferpool-wait-timebuffer.memory pressure — send() will start blocking (max.block.ms) when exhausted

Logging, tracing, and interceptors

  • Client logs (log4j/slf4j) surface warnings you should not ignore: rebalances, disconnections, expired transactions, slow commits.
  • Message headers carry correlation/trace IDs across the pipeline; standard practice is W3C/OpenTelemetry trace context propagated producer → consumer so one business transaction is traceable end to end.
  • Interceptors (ProducerInterceptor / ConsumerInterceptor) hook onSend/onConsume for cross-cutting telemetry (count, tag, trace) without touching business code — this is how Confluent Monitoring interceptors work.
  • End-to-end latency = event timestamp vs processing time at the consumer — measure it; per-hop metrics alone hide pipeline delays.

Troubleshooting common scenarios

SymptomLikely cause → check
Lag grows on ALL partitionsProcessing too slow overall → processing time per record, thread pool, downstream calls
Lag grows on ONE partitionHot key/skewed partitioning, or one stuck consumer → key distribution, that consumer's logs
Consumer keeps leaving the grouppoll() gaps exceed max.poll.interval.ms → reduce max.poll.records or speed up processing
Duplicates downstreamAt-least-once + rebalances/crashes between processing and commit → idempotent handling or EOS/transactions
Producer TimeoutException, buffer fullCluster slow/unreachable or produce rate > capacity → request latency, cluster health, buffer.memory
Consumer gets garbage / DeserializationExceptionSerializer/converter mismatch or schema change → producer serializer vs consumer deserializer, registry compatibility

Exam tip: consumer LAG is the metric the exam cares most about — know its definition, where to read it (metrics + kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe), and the remediation options. Producer-side, record-error-rate vs record-retry-rate (failed vs retried) is the classic pair. And every "consumer evicted from group" scenario resolves to max.poll.interval.ms.

cards.back: Ordered ONLY within a partition. Same key → same partition (murmur2(key) % numPartitions) → per-key ordering.

cards.front: Consumer group with more consumers than partitions?
cards.back: Extras sit IDLE — each partition goes to exactly one consumer per group. Partition count = max parallelism.
replication.factor=3, min.insync.replicas=2, producer acks=all — tolerates one broker down; every ack'd write on ≥2 replicas; NotEnoughReplicas if ISR < 2.
In-Sync Replicas: replicas fully caught up with the leader. New leaders are elected FROM the ISR (no data loss). Unclean leader election (non-ISR) is off by default.
cleanup.policy=compact keeps at least the LATEST value per key (changelog). A null-value record (tombstone) deletes the key after tombstone retention.
7 days (retention.ms) or retention.bytes — deletion regardless of consumption; Kafka is a log, not a queue.

cards.front: Adding partitions to a keyed topic — consequence?
cards.back: hash % numPartitions changes → keys REMAP; per-key ordering breaks across the change. Partitions can never be removed.

cards.front: ACLs needed to consume topic T with group G?
cards.back: READ on topic T AND READ on group G (offset commits are group operations).
KRaft — an internal Raft controller quorum manages cluster metadata (default since Kafka 4.0). Client APIs unchanged.
0: fire-and-forget. 1: leader wrote it (lost if leader dies pre-replication). all: all in-sync replicas have it (pair with min.insync.replicas).
Duplicates from the producer's own RETRIES (broker dedupes by sequence number), preserving per-partition order. NOT app-level re-sends, NOT consumer duplicates.
Transactions: transactional.id + initTransactions + beginTransaction + sendOffsetsToTransaction + commitTransaction; consumers read with isolation.level=read_committed.
linger.ms (wait to fill batches) + batch.size + compression.type (whole-batch: zstd/lz4/snappy/gzip) — bigger batches compress and ship better.
enable.auto.commit can commit offsets for records NOT yet processed → crash loses them. At-least-once = process first, then commitSync/commitAsync.

cards.front: Consumer keeps getting kicked from the group during slow processing — which setting?
cards.back: max.poll.interval.ms exceeded (time between poll() calls; default 5 min). Raise it or reduce max.poll.records. Heartbeats (session.timeout.ms) run on a background thread.
ONLY when the group has no valid committed offset (new group, expired, out-of-range). earliest/latest/none.
Magic byte + 4-byte SCHEMA ID + payload. Consumers fetch the writer's schema by ID from Schema Registry (cached).

cards.front: BACKWARD vs FORWARD compatibility — who upgrades first?
cards.back: BACKWARD (default): new schema reads OLD data → CONSUMERS first. FORWARD: old schema reads NEW data → PRODUCERS first. FULL: both.

cards.front: Retriable vs non-retriable producer errors — examples?
cards.back: Retriable: NotLeaderForPartition, NetworkException, NotEnoughReplicas (transient). Non-retriable: SerializationException, RecordTooLargeException, AuthorizationException.

cards.front: CLI to see a group's lag per partition?
cards.back: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group <name> → CURRENT-OFFSET, LOG-END-OFFSET, LAG (also --reset-offsets).
One TASK per input partition; tasks spread across threads/instances sharing application.id (= consumer group id). Instances beyond partition count idle.
KStream: every record an event. KTable: latest value per key (changelog). GlobalKTable: fully replicated to each instance; joins need NO co-partitioning.

cards.front: What backs a state store for fault tolerance?
cards.back: A compacted CHANGELOG topic; state is restored by replay; num.standby.replicas keeps warm copies for fast failover.
Tumbling: fixed, no overlap. Hopping: fixed size + smaller advance (overlap). Sliding: pairs within a time difference. Session: closed by inactivity gap.
processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 (wraps consume-process-produce + changelogs in transactions; brokers ≥ 2.5).

cards.front: Emit only the FINAL result per window?
cards.back: suppress(untilWindowCloses) — buffers intermediate updates until the window + grace period end.

cards.front: Connect distributed mode: where is state stored, how is it managed?
cards.back: Config/offsets/status in internal KAFKA topics; managed via the REST API; tasks rebalance across workers on failure. Standalone = local files, no fault tolerance.
Connector = logical job definition; TASKS are the parallel data-copying units (bounded by tasks.max and partitions/tables).

cards.front: Converters — role and the classic mistake?
cards.back: key/value.converter translate Connect records ↔ bytes (Avro/JSON Schema/Protobuf need schema.registry.url). Independent of the connector. Mismatch with the topic's actual format = garbage/deserialization errors.

cards.front: Connect error tolerance + DLQ — configs and limitation?
cards.back: errors.tolerance=all + errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name (+context.headers.enable). DLQs are a SINK connector feature.
CDC (Debezium) reads the DB transaction log: captures DELETEs, low latency, no table load. Polling misses deletes, loads the DB, latency = poll interval.

cards.front: Test tools by layer: client logic / Streams topology / real-broker concerns?
cards.back: MockProducer & MockConsumer / TopologyTestDriver (TestInputTopic, TestOutputTopic, state stores, time control) / Testcontainers or embedded Kafka.

cards.front: Prevent schema changes breaking consumers — where?
cards.back: In CI: validate candidate schemas against the registry's compatibility mode (REST check or schema-registry:test-compatibility Maven goal).
LOG-END-OFFSET − committed/current offset per partition. Watch records-lag-max metric or kafka-consumer-groups.sh; alert on SUSTAINED growth.
error-rate = sends that FAILED after retries (data loss — alert on non-zero). retry-rate = transient trouble being retried (early warning).

cards.front: Cross-service tracing through Kafka — where does trace context travel?
cards.back: In message HEADERS (W3C/OpenTelemetry context), injected on produce and extracted on consume — never in the key (would change partitioning).
